coredata.dc:title: Preparation of coaxial heterogeneous graphene quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays via linker molecule binding and electrophoretic deposition
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hui
Xing, Junheng
Xia, Zhengbin
Chen, Jiangqiong
coredata.dc:description: Coaxial heterogeneous graphene quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs/TNTs) are prepared by a coupling technique of linker molecule binding and electrophoretic deposition (EPD). The silane linker molecules act as a superb medium for integrating GQDs and TNTs by covalent amide linkage, thus preventing GQDs from clogging the tube entrances and forming a uniform GQD layer tightly attached to the inside tube walls during the following EPD process. By adjusting the time of EPD, appropriate thickness of the deposited GQDs in the internal tube walls of TNTs can be controlled. Compared to the pristine TNTs and GQDs/TNTs prepared by the conventional impregnation–precipitation method, the hybrids fabricated by EPD exhibit significantly enhanced photoelectrochemical water-splitting activity and photocatalytic organic dye decomposition performance for their broad photo-absorption range, fast separation of photogenerated charge, and stability.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of photocatalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, NO. 20976058 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the coaxial heterogeneous-structured GQDs/TNTs fabricated via coupling techniques of linker molecule binding and EPD. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of the GQDs. (b) AFM image of the GQDs deposited on Si substrate. (c) UV–vis absorption (ABS) and PL spectra of the aqueous GQDs solution excited at 340nm. Inset in (c) is a photograph of the GQDs aqueous solution under UV irradiation (365nm). (d) PL spectra of the GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (e) Height profile along the line A–B in (b). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images of samples: Top surface view of (a) the pristine TNTs, (b) 1-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, (c) 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, (e) 10-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, and (f) IP-GQDs/TNTs. (d) Cross-sectional view of 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs. The top right insets in (a) and (d) show the corresponding cross-sectional view and high-magnification view, respectively. The bottom left insets in (a), (b), (c), and (e) show a high-magnification view of the corresponding surface nanopores. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of individual tube for various samples: (a) pure TNTs. (b) 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs. (c) IP-GQDs/TNTs.
FTIR spectra of (a) pristine TNTs, (b) KH550-modified TNTs, (c) IP-GQDs/TNTs, and (d) EPD-GQDs/TNTs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic comparison of GQD/TNT junctions formed by a method of coupling techniques of linker molecule binding and EPD, conventional IP process, and directly EPD. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman spectra of (a) pure TNTs and (b) EPD-GQDs/TNTs. The inset shows the magnification of Raman shift ranging from 1200 to 1700cm−1. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
C 1s XPS spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) EPD-GQDs/TNTs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of pure TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photoelectrochemical properties of the prepared samples under illumination of stimulated solar light (AM 1.5, 100mW/cm2): (a) amperometric I–t curves at an applied potential of 1.23V (vs. RHE), (b) linear-sweep voltammograms with a scanning rate of 5mV/s, and (c) photoconversion efficiency as a function of applied potentials. (d) IPCE plots collected at an incident wavelength range from 300 to 500nm without an external bias; the inset is the magnified IPCE spectra at an incident wavelength range from 400 to 500nm. (e) Scheme showing the PEC water-splitting process of the EPD-GQD/TNT photoanode-based PEC cells. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Visible-light-induced PC degradation rate curves and (b) corresponding kinetic curves of MB using pure TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs as photocatalysts. (c) The recycled degradation of MB for IP-GQDs/TNTs and EPD-GQDs/TNTs under visible light irradiation. (d) PL spectra of pure TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs in a 5×10−4 M TA under visible light illumination for 30min. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Proposed mechanism for the PC degradation of MB by the coaxial heterogeneous-structured GQDs/TNTs under visible light irradiation. The energy level of photoexcited MB, GQDs, and the CB of anatase TNTs are −3.6, −3.7, and −4.2eV (vs. vacuum), respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Preparation of coaxial heterogeneous graphene quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays via linker molecule binding and electrophoretic deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510641, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coaxial heterogeneous graphene quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs/TNTs) are prepared by a coupling technique of linker molecule binding and electrophoretic deposition (EPD). The silane linker molecules act as a superb medium for integrating GQDs and TNTs by covalent amide linkage, thus preventing GQDs from clogging the tube entrances and forming a uniform GQD layer tightly attached to the inside tube walls during the following EPD process. By adjusting the time of EPD, appropriate thickness of the deposited GQDs in the internal tube walls of TNTs can be controlled. Compared to the pristine TNTs and GQDs/TNTs prepared by the conventional impregnation–precipitation method, the hybrids fabricated by EPD exhibit significantly enhanced photoelectrochemical water-splitting activity and photocatalytic organic dye decomposition performance for their broad photo-absorption range, fast separation of photogenerated charge, and stability.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the energy crisis and environmental pollution have become two key problems that hinder social progress. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting for hydrogen production and photocatalytic (PC) decomposition of toxic organic pollutants are expected to be effective routes for tackling these problems. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been served as one of the most promising photocatalysts due to its superior PC activity, high thermal and chemical stability, nontoxicity, and low cost [1–2]. Among all of the TiO2 nanostructured materials, vertically aligned TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNTs) have gained considerable attention as they can offer a large interior and outer surface area and provide a direct pathway for the transfer of photoexcited electrons, which causes the enhancement of light absorption and carrier separation [3,4]. In addition, the oriented nanotube structure can reduce the loss of light reflection under illumination, as the exterior photons that enter the internal nanotubes are less likely to escape because of multiple radiation scattering by the walls [5]. Thus far, various methods, such as hydrothermal processes [6,7], nanoporous alumina template methods [8], electrodeposition [9], and microwave irradiation [10], have been employed for preparing TNTs. However, these methods are often retarded by the problems of tedious operation procedures, intricate template removal, and poor adhesion of nanotube arrays with the underlying substrate. By contrast, the electrochemical anodization of Ti foil has been extensively adopted to fabricate highly ordered, vertically oriented TNTs owing to their simple preparation, and the prepared TNTs have good mechanical adhesion, electronic conductivity, and controllable morphologies [11–14]. Nevertheless, the PEC and PC efficiency of the TNTs utilizing sunlight is relatively low as a result of the limited optical response to ultraviolet (UV) light and the fast recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Therefore, extending the optical absorption to the visible light region and suppressing the recombination of photocarriers are the two key ways to improve the PEC and PC activity of the TNTs.

Up to date, various attempts have been made to modify the anodized TNTs. Doping TNTs with nonmetal elements (such as N [15], B [16], and F [17]) and coupling TNTs with noble metals (such as Au [18–20], Ag [21–23], Pd [24,25], and Pt [26]) have been demonstrated to be two promising ways for boosting the separation of photocarriers or broadening the light absorption further to the visible region. However, the dopants of the doped TNTs partly serve as carrier recombination centers, which undermines the PC performance of the doped TNTs [27]. The loaded noble metals of the coupled TNTs are easily oxidized at the metal–TiO2 interface and eventually form a thin metal-oxide layer at room temperature, thus possibly leading to the PC ability of the multiple materials being impaired [28]. In recent years, TNTs sensitized with narrow-bandgap semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (such as CdS [29,30], CdSe [31], and PbS [32]) have attracted much attention as the semiconductor QDs possess tunable bandgap by sizes, higher extinction coefficients than traditional dyes, and multiple exciton generation [31,33–35]. For the semiconductor QD-sensitized TNTs, the visible light can be fully used and the recombination of photocarriers can be effectively suppressed, therefore resulting in significant improvement of the PEC and PC activity of the TNTs. However, most of these semiconductor QD sensitizers are either highly unstable during light irradiation or poisonous, thus limiting their practical applications [36,37]. The exploration of a harmless, low-cost QD photosensitizer is highly demanded for coupling with TNTs to further enhance their PEC and PC activity.

Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist of single- or few-layer graphene with a size of only several nanometers, standing for a new type of QDs with unique properties combining the graphene nature and size-resulted quantum effects [38,39], which seem to be a promising QD sensitizer for decorating wide-bandgap semiconductors. Numerous simple methods have been adopted for preparing GQDs, including hydrothermal [40], solvothermal [41], electrochemical [42,43], and microwave [44] methods. These mild, low-cost, and green synthetic methods are highly suitable for the mass production of GQDs. It has been demonstrated that GQDs can show continuous absorption in the UV–visible light (vis) region because of the overlap of electronic absorption bands induced by closely spaced electronic energy levels and vibronic coupling [39]. Moreover, the hot carriers of GQDs possess considerably long lifetimes, thus potentially favoring hot-carrier harvesting and multi-excitation generation that can exceed the Shockley–Queisser limit for efficient solar energy utilization [45]. As a result, GQDs can act as an ideal photosensitizer for coupling semiconductor materials. In addition, GQDs have rich functional groups, such as carboxylic acid moieties at the edge, thus rendering them excellent water solubility and functionalized possibilities [39]. Recently, GQD-sensitized TNTs (GQDs/TNTs) have been prepared by electrophoretic deposition (EPD) or the impregnation–precipitation (IP) method, and they exhibit a significantly higher PEC activity than pristine TNTs [46,47]. However, direct deposition methods such as EPD or IP easily lead to clogging of the pores before the insides of the TNTs can be uniformly decorated with GQDs, thus preventing full utilization of the internal area of the TNTs towards forming intimate GQD/TNT heterogeneous junctions. Lately, a large amount of reports have demonstrated the linker-assisted attachment of QDs to the surface of TiO2 for forming a self-assembled monolayer, which can effectively suppress the formation of bulk QD layers on the deposited substrate. However, most of the reports concentrated on CdS QDs and the bifunctional linker molecules of the type HS–R–COOH [48–50], and to the best of our knowledge, few reports focus on the silane coupling agent for attaching GQDs to TiO2. Hence, a judicious combination of GQDs and TNTs through silane linker molecules leading to a well-defined hybrid heterostructure as an efficient photocatalyst is highly desired.

Herein, we construct unique coaxial heterogeneous-structured GQDs/TNTs by a coupling technique of silane linker molecule modification and EPD. As shown in Fig. 1 , highly ordered top-porous TNTs are firstly synthesized by a two-step anodization method. Then, 3-aminopropropyltriethoxysilane (denoted as KH550) is filled into the TNTs by capillary force. The amine groups of the KH550 can covalently integrate with the carboxylic acid groups of the electrophoretic GQDs to form amide groups, thus achieving the combination of TNTs and GQDs to produce firmly coalescent heterojunctions of GQDs/TNTs. It is worth noting that the KH550 modification for semiconductors does not affect the absorption behavior of the semiconductors [51]; thus, for testing the PEC and PC of the GQD/TNT hybrid, we can exclude the impact of KH550. By adjusting the time of EPD, the aggregation of GQDs on the surface of TNTs can be prevented and the appropriate thickness of the deposited GQD layer in the internal tube walls can be controlled. The photosensitizer of GQDs coupled with TNTs can significantly enhance the PEC and PC activity of pure TNTs. Furthermore, the GQDs/TNTs prepared by the EPD method show a great PEC and PC performance compared to the GQDs/TNTs prepared by the IP method. The mechanism for GQD-enabled performance enhancement is discussed in detail.

GQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal cutting method as described before [40]. In brief, graphene oxide (GO) sheets were acquired from graphite powder by the Hummers method. Graphene sheets (GSs) were obtained by the thermal deoxidation of GO sheets in a tube furnace at 280°C for 2h with a heating and cooling rate of 5°C/min in a nitrogen atmosphere. Then the GSs were subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 12h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting suspension was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3500Da); thus, the GQDs were obtained. Before their use, the GQDs were dissolved in an aqueous solution and ultrasonicated for 10min.

Top-porous and highly ordered TNTs were prepared by a two-step anodization method similar to previous reports [52,53]. Briefly, a two-electrode electrochemical cell, with a working electrode of Ti foil and a counter electrode of Pt foil, was used for the preparation of TNTs. The titanium foils (purity 99.6%, thickness 1.0mm) were firstly anodized in ethylene glycol (EG) electrolyte containing 0.5wt% NH4F and 2vol% deionized water under 60V for 2h. Then the as-prepared TiO2 layer was peeled off by ultrasonication in deionized water. Subsequently, the well-textured titanium foils were subjected to a second anodization in the same electrolyte at 60V for 1h. Finally, the as-anodized TNTs were annealed at 450°C for 2h.

EPD and IP were used to prepare GQD/TNT heterojunctions. For the EPD hybrid method, the as-prepared TNTs were firstly immersed in 5.0wt% 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (denoted as KH550) in an ethanol solution (95% ethanol with trace water) for 3h under magnetic stirring condition at room temperature. Then a three-electrode cell – with the working electrode of KH550-modified TNTs, the counter electrode of a Pt foil, and the reference electrode of KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl, an aqueous electrolyte of 0.1mg/mL GQDs (pH=9) – was employed to integrate the GQDs with the modified TNTs by EPD at 4V for various deposition times (1, 5, and 10min). For a comparison, the conventional IP method was also adopted to combine the GQDs with the modified TNTs for 10min. The as-prepared GQDs/TNTs were rinsed with ethanol.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hitachi H-7650, Tokyo, Japan) was used to observe the size distribution of GQDs and the morphology details of individual tubes. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained in the tapping mode on a Nanoscope IIIa (Digital Instrument). Absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The morphologies of the samples were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (NOVA NANOSEM 430, Eindhoven, the Netherlands) at an acceleration voltage of 15kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an FTIR (Perkin Elmer, 2200) spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Bremen, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation and operating at 40kV/40mA. The Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM Aramis (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, Paris, France) instrument, which was performed with the incidence power of 10.4mW and the excitation wavelength of 532nm. The UV–vis light absorption spectra were obtained from a Hitachi UV-3010 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with an integrating sphere assembly and using BaSO4 as a reference to measure all the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Manchester, UK) was used to detect the chemical composition of the as-prepared samples. The spectral positions were corrected by normalizing the C 1s spectrum at 284.6eV, and a Shirley background was used for the peak fitting. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000). The excitation wavelength was 420nm, with a scanning speed of 600nm/min. The widths of the excitation slit and emission slit were 2.5 and 5.0nm, respectively.

PEC measurements were carried out in 1M KOH electrolyte with a standard three-electrode cell of the obtained sample, platinum foil, and Ag/AgCl as the working, counter, and reference electrode, respectively. The potential and photocurrent of the photoelectrode were controlled by a potentiostat and were reported against the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE):
(1)
E
RHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.059
pH +
E
Ag / AgCl
°
with
E
Ag / AgCl
°
= 0.1976
V
at
25 °
C

The samples were illuminated by a stimulated solar light (AM1.5, 100mW/cm2) provided by a PLS-SXE300UV Xe lamp (Changtuo, China). The photocurrent response under zero bias was recorded with an Agilent digital multimeter. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were performed without external bias in a two-electrode model, with prepared samples as the anode and Pt as the cathode. All experiments were carried out under ambient conditions.

The PC activity was measured in an XPA II reactor (Nanjing Xujiang Machine-electronic Plant, Nanjing, China). The samples (13×10mm) were immersed in 15-mL quartz test tubes containing 5mL methylene blue (MB, 10mg/L) in the dark for 1h to achieve adsorption equilibrium before radiation. After that, a 1000-W Xe lamp (the UV light was cut off by 2.0M sodium nitrite) was used as the visible light source to conduct the PC experiment. The remaining MB concentration in the reaction solution was determined using the UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Japan).

The formation of hydroxyl radicals (OH) at the photoilluminated samples/water interface can be detected by the PL method using terephthalic acid (TA) as a molecule probe. TA can quickly react with OH to generate 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, whose fluorescence intensity is proportional to the amount of OH generated in water. The experimental steps are similar to the measurement of PC except that the MB aqueous solution is replaced by 5×10−4 M TA and 2×10−3 M NaOH. After photoreaction of 30min, the fluorescence spectra of the resultant solution were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal cutting method as described elsewhere [40]. The TEM image displays the prepared GQDs are collected monodisperse (Fig. 2 a). Their lateral sizes are mainly in the range of 2–16nm (9nm average) (Fig. S1a). AFM images show that their topographic heights are between 0.5 and 2.5nm, indicating that most of the obtained GQDs are one to three layers (Figs. 2b, e and S1b). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs in aqueous solution shows an absorption band at 340nm and the PL spectrum shows a strong peak at 460nm (Fig. 2c). The PL emission quantum yield at 340nm using quinine sulfate as a reference is 7.6%, which is slightly higher than that of reported GQDs [40]. In addition, the GQDs in water emit blue PL under an excitation of 365nm (Fig. 2c, inset), which can probably be attributed to the effect of size and surface functional groups. Similar to previous reports, the GQDs also exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior [40]. When the excitation wavelength is varied from 340 to 440nm, the PL peak shifts to longer wavelengths with the strongest peak at 460nm when excited at 340nm (Fig. 2d).

The SEM images of the bare TNTs, the GQDs/TNTs prepared by linker molecule binding and the EPD method (denoted as EPD-GQDs/TNTs), and the GQDs/TNTs fabricated by linker molecule modification and the IP method (denoted as IP-GQDs/TNTs) are shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3a, the TNTs possess top hexagonal porous morphology and an underlying nanotubular structure. They are highly ordered, compactly arranged, and vertically aligned with a rather smooth and neat surface, a moderate tube length of approximately 10μm (Fig. 3a, inset), and an inner diameter of about 120nm, which is large enough to incorporate the GQDs into the TNTs.

The top-porous netlike structure can prevent nanotube bundling and inhibit the deposition of GQDs between the gaps of TNTs, thus making it possible to achieve EPD of GQDs only inside the tubes and to allow the formation of coaxial nanohybrids. To investigate the effect of the EPD time on the morphology of GQDs/TNTs, the EPD time is controlled at 1, 5, and 10min. The samples obtained correspondingly are denoted as 1-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, and 10-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs. Fig. 3b shows the coaxial heterogeneous-structured 1-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs with an inner diameter of about 100nm, which indicates that the thickness of the superthin GQD coating layer uniformly decorating the inside tube walls of TNTs is about 10nm. As the time of EPD increases to 5min, the internal diameter of the 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs decreases from 100 to 90nm (Fig. 3c). In addition, their tube walls are not clean and neat compared to the pristine TNTs (Fig. 3a, inset), which means that GQDs are embedded into the TNTs (Fig. 3d). When the time of EPD is up to 10min, the inside diameter of the 10-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs remains about 90nm and many GQDs cover the surface of TNTs (Fig. 3e), which may be because the EPD process in the pores of TNTs has reached its “saturated state” under the constant potential and the charged GQDs cannot proceed further into the narrowed pores. By contrast, for the IP-GQDs/TNTs, a serious aggregation of GQDs randomly distributed on the surface of TNTs suggests that poor junctions exist between GQDs and TNTs (Fig. 3f). The phenomenon of the average internal pore diameter of GQDs/TNTs decreasing with the increase of EPD time is further confirmed by statistical calculation (Fig. S2). As for the 1-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, their inner pore diameters vary from 95 to 105nm and about 62% locate at 100nm. When the deposition time is extended to 5min, the internal pore diameter obviously decreases and about 67% lie at 90nm. However, for the 10-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs, only about 43% of their inner pore diameter locate at 90nm, suggesting that the pore diameter remains relatively stable and their distribution uniformity becomes worse compared to that of the 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs.

In order to investigate the distribution of GQDs deposited in TNTs more thoroughly, TEM was explored to observe the samples of pristine TNTs, EPD-GQDs/TNTs (the following discussion involving EPD-GQDs/TNTs refers to the 5-min-EPD-GQDs/TNTs), and IP-GQDs/TNTs. Fig. 4 a shows the TEM image of an individual nanotube of the pure TNTs obtained after ultrasonication. The inner diameter and the wall thickness of the pure TNTs are about 120 and 15nm, respectively. It can be noticed that the internal wall of the pure TNTs is smooth and clean, which is in accordance with the SEM image (Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 4b, the GQD coating layer of 15nm is firmly attached to the internal tube wall of TNTs, which further confirms that the EPD-GQDs/TNTs have a unique coaxial heterogeneous structure. For the sample of GQDs/TNTs fabricated by IP for 10min, GQD clusters mainly clog the pore mouths of TNTs (Fig. 4c).

Fig. 5 shows the FTIR results of pure TNTs, KH550-modified TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs. In the spectrum of KH550-modified TNTs, the peak at approximately 1571cm−1 is the characteristic of the NH bond of the amine groups. For the spectrum of IP-GQDs/TNTs, the peak at about 1730cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretch of the carboxylic groups that stem from the GQDs [54]. The spectrum of EPD-GQDs/TNTs shows the disappearance of the peak at 1730cm−1 and the corresponding appearance of peaks at about 1640 and 1260cm−1 assigned to the amide carboxyl (CO) stretch and the amide C–N stretch, respectively [55]. These results clearly indicate that, for the EPD-GQDs/TNTs, GQDs are covalently bonded to the KH550 molecules by the amide linkage and attached to the TNTs; by contrast, for the IP-GQDs/TNTs, GQDs are just attached to the TNTs by noncovalent combination.

Thus, we propose the formation process of the coaxial heterogeneous GQDs/TNTs. A schematic illustration of GQD/TNT junctions formed by the EPD and IP methods can be seen in Fig. 6 . Firstly, the silane linker molecule KH550 are filled into the TNTs because of the capillary force, and closely attached to the internal walls of the hydrophilic TNTs forming a self-assembled monolayer due to the reaction of linker molecules with trace water to form the strong hydrophilic KH550 (denoted as H-KH550) with substantial hydroxyl groups. During the subsequent EPD process, the negatively charged GQDs can be quickly driven to the positive electrode and injected into the TNTs by an applied electric field. The amine groups of the H-KH550 fixed on the internal tube walls can covalently react with the carboxylic acid groups of the GQDs to form amide groups as the electric field force boosts the condensation of the amine groups and the carboxylic acid groups, thus tightly combining the TNTs with the GQDs to form coaxial heterogeneous-structured GQD/TNT junctions. By contrast, without the molecule KH550 modification, the pure TNTs are coupled with the GQDs directly by the EPD method; the GQDs are randomly distributed on the surface of TNTs and partly deposited in the inside TNTs by noncovalent binding (Fig. S3). This further confirms the important role of linker molecule KH550 in forming uniform and tight GQD/TNT heterojunctions. On the other hand, for the IP method, due to lack of enough power to incorporate the GQDs into the inside tubes, most of the GQDs only deposit on the surface of TNTs forming a crowd of undesired clusters that seriously clog the pores of TNTs.

Raman spectroscopy was used to confirm the existence of GQDs on the surface of TNTs. Fig. 7 displays that the peaks located at about 148, 396, 516, and 635cm−1, which can be assigned to the Eg, B1g, B1g or A1g, and Eg modes of the anatase phase [56], respectively, can be observed in both pristine TNTs and GQDs/TNTs. Moreover, for the GQDs/TNTs, the two additional peaks centered at 1365 and 1596cm−1 are considered to be the characteristic D band and G band of GQDs [57]. The D peak originates from the disruption of symmetrical hexagonal graphitic lattice due to the internal structural defects or edge defects, and the G peak is the response of the stretching motion of a symmetric sp2 CC bond [58]. Such evidence proves that the GQDs are actually deposited on the TNTs.

XPS C 1s spectra of the prepared GQDs and GQDs/TNTs are displayed in Fig. 8 . For the bare GQDs, four peaks at 284.6, 286.5, 287.5, and 288.8eV, correspond to CC, CO in hydroxyl or epoxy groups, CO, and OCO, respectively (Fig. 8a) [59,60]. After EPD, CC and CO remain unchanged, while CO and OCO disappear. In addition, a new peak arises at 286.0eV, which is attributed to CN (Fig. 8b). These results may be related to the introduction of KH550. The CO and OCO of GQDs completely react with the amino group of KH550 during the EPD process, and the CN may come from the formed amide groups or partially residual amino groups of KH550. Therefore, the important role of linker molecule KH550 in a combination of GQDs and TNTs is indirectly confirmed.

Fig. 9 shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra (DRS) of the pure TNTs, EPD-GQDs/TNTs, and IP-GQDs/TNTs. An obvious red shift of the absorption edge and stronger absorption in the visible range are observed for the GQDs/TNTs in comparison with the pristine TNTs, in which the EPD-GQDs/TNTs exhibit an enhanced absorbance compared to the IP-GQDs/TNTs. These results are potent to explain the enhanced PEC and PC performance of the GQD/TNT hybrid, as discussed later.

To test the PEC response of TNTs, EPD-GQDs/TNTs, and IP-GQDs/TNTs under AM 1.5 (100mW/cm2), the photocurrent densities measured in the light-on/light-off cycles under the bias of 0.23V vs. Ag/AgCl (1.23V vs. RHE) are shown in Fig. 10 a. The photocurrent densities are about 1.2, 3.4, and 5.0mA/cm2 for the bare TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs, respectively. The GQDs/TNTs exhibit much higher photocurrent densities than the pure TNTs, which indicates that the GQDs play an important role in enhancing the PEC activity of TNTs. Furthermore, the photocurrent density of EPD-GQDs/TNTs is 47% more than that of IP-GQDs/TNTs. The reason may be attributed to the fact that, for the EPD-GQDs/TNTs, GQDs are uniformly and firmly deposited on the inside tube walls, thus forming a coaxial heterogeneous structure for improving the separation of photocarriers. I–V characteristics (Fig. 10b) further confirm the superior performance of the produced higher photocurrents for the GQD/TNT composites over those of pristine TNTs. The open circuit potential (V oc) is around 0.15V vs. RHE for both EPD-GQDs/TNTs and IP-GQDs/TNTs, which is more negative than that of obtained TNTs alone (0.25V vs. RHE), demonstrating a shift in the Fermi level to a more negative potential as a result of coupling between TNTs and GQDs in the GQD/TNT hybrids. The hydrogen production efficiency of the prepared samples is calculated via the following equation [61]:
(2)
ε
0
( % ) =
j
p
E
rev
0
- |
E
app
|
I
0
× 100
where ε 0 is the photoconversion efficiency, j p is the photocurrent density (mA/cm2), I 0 means the intensity of the incident light, E 0 rev represents the standard reversible potential (1.23V vs. RHE), and |E app| denotes the absolute value of the applied voltage, which is obtained from E app = E meas − E aoc, where E meas is the electrode potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) at which j p is measured and E aoc is the electrode potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) at open circuit under illumination. The plots of photoconversion efficiency with applied potential are shown in Fig. 10c. The maximum photoconversion efficiency of 1.66% and 2.54% is achieved for IP-GQDs/TNTs and EPD-GQDs/TNTs, respectively, at 0.7V vs. RHE, at least three times higher than that of pure TNTs (0.57% at 0.7V vs. RHE). IPCE measurements are performed on the prepared TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs. The IPCE values can be calculated by the following equation [62]:
(3)
IPCE = ( 12701 ) / λ
J
light
where I, λ, and J light are the photocurrent density, incident light wavelength, and measured irradiance, respectively. To explore the photoinduced charge transfer separation and transfer process, no external bias potential is applied during the IPCE measurements. Fig. 10d shows that the IPCE of GQD/TNT-based PEC devices has larger values for all wavelengths (300–500nm) compared to TNT-based devices, which agrees well with the PEC results. In the UV region, the EPD-GQDs/TNTs and IP-GQDs/TNTs have the IPCE values of 86.4% and 72.8% at 300nm, respectively, which is 2.1 and 1.8 times higher than that of pristine TNTs. It indicates that the UV light is effectively utilized for PEC water splitting because of the enhanced carrier separation and transfer induced by the loaded GQDs. Notably, the GQDs/TNTs show significant photoactivity improvement within the visible light region (400–500nm) compared to the pristine TNTs (inset of Fig. 10d), which can be attributed to strong visible light absorption of the green sensitizer GQDs. In addition, the EPD-GQDs/TNTs exhibit higher IPCE values over the entire measured wavelength range, indicating that the coaxial heterostructure actually facilitates the light absorption and separation of photogenerated carriers. To further understand the mechanism of PEC water-splitting performance enhancement by coupling GQDs, Fig. 10e schematically shows the PEC water-splitting process with GQD/TNT photoanode-based PEC cells. According to the literature [63–65], anatase TiO2 generally has a bandgap of 3.2eV with a conducting band (CB) of −4.2eV and a valence band (VB) of −7.4eV vs. vacuum. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the GQDs (9nm average) are −3.7 and −5.4eV, respectively. For the GQD/TNT photoanode under AM 1.5 (100mW/cm2), the electrons on the HOMO of GQDs are firstly excited to the LUMO of GQDs, and then the photogenerated electrons are quickly injected into the CB of TNTs due to the CB of TNTs being lower than the LUMO of GQDs. At the same time, certain electrons can also be excited from the VB to CB of TNTs because of a small part of the UV light of sunlight. All of the excited electrons flow to the counter Pt electrode for hydrogen production. Moreover, the coaxial heterogeneous character of GQDs/TNTs can significantly boost photoexcited charge separation. The separated holes of TNTs can enter the HOMO of GQDs and then transfer to the interface between the GQD/TNT photoanode and the aqueous electrolyte, subsequently reacting with water to engender oxygen.

The PC activity of the samples was measured by PC degradation of MB solution under visible light illumination. After 2h of visible light irradiation, about 37%, 56%, and 80% of MB was decomposed by TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs, respectively (Fig. 11 a). The PC degradation of MB is a pseudo-first-order reaction and its kinetics can be expressed as follows [66]:
(4)
ln (
C
0
/ C ) = kt
where k is the apparent rate constant, t is illumination time, and C 0 and C are the initial concentration and the reaction concentration of MB, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11b, the rate constant of pristine TNTs, IP-GQDs/TNTs, and EPD-GQDs/TNTs is 0.01329, 0.00691, and 0.01329min−1, respectively. The above results reveal that the GQDs/TNTs show higher PC activity than the pristine TNTs because the GQDs possess the advantages of having excellent absorption capacity of MB and a broad visible light absorption range, and being a superb mediator of charge separation and transfer. Furthermore, the PC performance of the IP-GQDs/TNTs is inferior to that of the EPD-GQDs/TNTs. Therefore, for the IP-GQDs/TNTs, we propose that most of the GQDs are loosely deposited on the surface of TNTs, thus leading to high charge recombination and partial redissolution of the GQDs in water, which is confirmed by the cycling stability experiments of photocatalysts (Fig. 11c). After five cycles for the PC decomposition of MB, the PC activity of the IP-GQDs/TNTs suffers from severe loss up to 28%. Nevertheless, the EPD-GQDs/TNTs remain stable with a negligible loss <1%. This high cycling stability of the EPD-GQDs/TNTs is ascribed to the firm bonding between the GQDs and TNTs by the coupling methods of linker molecule KH550 modification and EPD. To illustrate the relationship between carrier separation and PC properties in the prepared samples, a TA fluorescence probe was used to measure the level of hydroxyl radicals (OH) generated in the reaction system. TA can quickly react with OH to generate 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, whose fluorescence intensity is proportional to the amount of OH [67,68]. Fig. 11d shows the comparison of fluorescence intensity for the prepared samples, which have been illuminated under visible light for 30min. It can be seen that the fluorescent intensities of the IP-GQDs/TNTs and the EPD-GQDs/TNTs are much higher than that of the pristine TNTs, which can be ascribed to the fact that the green sensitizer GQDs can not only absorb visible light for substantial photoexcited electron–hole pair production but also facilitate these charge separations. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the EPD-GQDs/TNTs is about two times that of the IP-GQDs/TNTs, thus indirectly indicating that the uniform GQD layer can greatly improve the separation of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs produced. The hydroxyl radical experimental results confirm that the hydroxyl radicals indeed participate in the PC reactions and can further explain the different PC activities of the prepared samples.

To further clarify the function of GQDs in the GQD/TNT PEC and PC systems, PL was used to reveal the differences in the PEC and PC efficiencies of photocatalysts because PL emission originates from the recombination of free carriers [69,70]. All samples display the PL peaks at around 540nm (Fig. S5). Obviously, the pure TNTs show strong PL signals. The introduction of GQDs leads to a significant decrease in the emission intensity of the PL spectrum, compared with that of pristine TNTs, suggesting a much lower recombination rate of photoexcited electron–hole pairs, which demonstrates that the GQDs sensitizers can serve as an effective electron mediator and hinder the direct recombination of photoinduced carriers in TNTs. Moreover, the EPD-GQDs/TNTs exhibit weaker PL intensity than the IP-GQDs/TNTs, indicating that the uniform and firm junctions of GQDs and TNTs facilitate the separation of photogenerated carriers.

According to the above PC, PL, and hydroxyl radical experimental results, we attribute the significantly enhanced PC activity of the coaxial heterogeneous-structured EPD-GQDs/TNTs to three aspects, including the high absorption capacity of pollutants, a broad photo-absorption range, and fast separation of the photogenerated charge. The possible PC decomposition mechanism of MB for the EPD-GQDs/TNTs has been proposed and is schematically illustrated in Fig. 12 . The MB dye layer is firmly attached to the GQD sensitizer layer due to the strong π–π conjugated interaction between the benzene rings of MB and the surface of GQDs [71]. Under visible light illumination, the electrons on the HOMO (−5.2eV) of MB are excited to the LUMO (−3.6eV) of MB, and then the excited electrons flow to the LUMO of GQDs because the LUMO of GQDs is slightly smaller than that of MB. Meanwhile, the sensitizer GQDs can also be excited to generate many electrons under visible light irradiation. The excited electrons stored on the LUMO of GQDs rapidly flow to the CB of the TNTs. Finally, all the excited electrons can be trapped by oxygen molecules in the aqueous solution to produce the superoxide anion O2, while the photogenerated holes can oxidize the MB molecules into end products through the demethylation route [72]. Moreover, tight and uniform contact between GQDs and TNTs further promotes the charge separation for improving the PC performance of GQD/TNT hybrids.

A green sensitizer of GQDs has been employed to integrate with the top-porous TNTs by a combined technique of linker molecule KH550 binding and EPD. This method effectively promotes the deposition of GQDs on the internal tube walls and preventing GQD blocking at the tube entrances, thus enabling the formation of coaxial heterogeneous-structured GQD/TNT junctions. The appropriate thickness (about 10nm) of the electrophoretic-deposited GQD layer can be achieved at 5min. The GQDs/TNTs prepared by EPD and IP are superior to the pristine TNTs in terms of PEC and PC performance, which can be ascribed to the characteristics of a broad photo-absorption range and fast separation of the photogenerated charge of the integrated GQDs. Furthermore, the EPD-GQDs/TNTs show greatly enhanced PC activity and stability in comparison with the IP-GQDs/TNTs due to the uniform and tight junctions between the GQDs and the TNTs. Thus, the EPD-GQDs/TNTs provide a promising photoanode for PEC water splitting and a superb photocatalyst for the PC decomposition of organic pollutants. The method for preparing GQD/TNT hybrids can be extended to construct high-quality coupling materials based on GQDs and other one-dimensional semiconductors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, NO. 20976058).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.09.080.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document contains Supplementary Figs. S1–S5.
